title: Energy conservation
summary: Cambridge 9702 Paper 2 — Energy conservation (5.1). Senpai Corner diagram-backed pilot with premium structure and live visuals.
learningObjectives: Apply the principle of energy conservation to solve problems.
Calculate kinetic and gravitational potential energy changes using relevant formulas.
Understand and calculate work done, power, and efficiency in various physical systems.

simpleExplanation.title: Energy: The Ultimate Shape-Shifter!
simpleExplanation.summary: Energy is never truly gone; it just changes its form. Imagine your phone battery losing charge – that energy doesn't disappear; it powers the screen, creates sound, and generates some heat, all different forms of energy.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a playground slide. When you're at the top, you have lots of 'slide potential energy'. As you slide down, that potential energy turns into 'sliding motion energy' (kinetic energy). Even if your bottom gets a bit warm due to friction, the total energy involved in your slide is conserved, just transformed.
steps: Identify all forms of energy present at the start and end (e.g., kinetic, potential, internal).
Account for energy transformations between these forms (e.g., GPE converting to KE).
Consider any energy 'losses' to unwanted forms, like heat from friction or air resistance.
Apply the fundamental principle: total energy before = total energy after, in a closed system.

flashcards.front: What is the fundamental principle of energy conservation in a closed system?
flashcards.back: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed, so the total energy remains constant.

flashcards.front: How is kinetic energy dependent on an object's mass and velocity?
flashcards.back: It is directly proportional to mass and the square of velocity ($KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$).

flashcards.front: Under what condition is work done on an object?
flashcards.back: Work is done only if a force causes displacement in the direction of the applied force.

flashcards.front: What does an efficiency less than 1 (or 100%) imply for a real system?
flashcards.back: It implies some input energy is inevitably lost, typically as heat or sound, rather than being converted into useful output.

flashcards.front: Can the reference point for calculating gravitational potential energy be changed?
flashcards.back: Yes, the reference point can be chosen arbitrarily, as only the change in vertical height ($\Delta h$) is significant.

flashcards.front: What is the formula for work done when force and displacement are in the same direction?
Define power in terms of energy and time.
flashcards.back: Power is the rate at which energy is transferred or work is performed ($P = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta t}$).

flashcards.front: How can power be calculated if you know the force and velocity?
What is meant by a 'closed system' in the context of energy conservation?
flashcards.back: A system where no matter or energy can enter or leave from its surroundings.

flashcards.front: What happens to energy 'lost' due to air resistance?
flashcards.back: It is converted into internal energy (heat and sound) of the object and the surroundings.

flashcards.front: What is the formula for Gravitational Potential Energy (GPE)?

sections.content: Ever wondered why a bouncing ball eventually stops? Or how a rollercoaster gains incredible speed? It all comes down to **energy conservation** – a fundamental principle in Physics that explains how energy transforms from one form to another without ever being lost or created. This pilot aligns with **Senpai Corner** AS notes (2025–2027) for topic **5.1**.
The Unbreakable Rule: Conservation of Energy

At its core, the **Principle of Conservation of Energy** states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed. Instead, it continuously changes from one form to another. In a **closed system**, where no energy can enter or leave, the total amount of energy always stays constant.

items: In physics, work (W) is the energy transferred to or from an object through the application of force (F) along a displacement (d) 𝑊 = 𝐹 × 𝑑.
The SI units for work is in Joules.
The principle of conservation of energy states that energy is neither created nor destroyed. But may transform from one type to another .
E.g. work can be transformed to heat (friction!), electric to light.
Not all energy transferred is useful. E.g. when transferring electric to light, some energy is wasted in the form of heat!.

sections.content: Energy's Many Forms: Kinetic and Gravitational Potential

Two crucial forms of energy we frequently encounter are **Kinetic Energy (KE)** and **Gravitational Potential Energy (GPE)**. KE is the energy an object possesses due to its motion, while GPE is the energy stored due to its position in a gravitational field.
Kinetic energy depends on an object's mass ($m$) and the square of its velocity ($v$). The faster or more massive an object, the more kinetic energy it has.

Gravitational potential energy depends on mass ($m$), gravitational acceleration ($g$), and vertical height ($h$). You can choose any convenient reference point for 'h', as only the *change* in height matters for $\Delta GPE$.

items: Kinetic energy is related to an object's movement.
GPE is energy stored by an object's height in a gravitational field.
KE is proportional to mass and the square of velocity.
GPE depends on mass, gravity, and vertical height change.
The GPE reference height can be set arbitrarily.

sections.content: Work, Power, and Efficiency: Energy in Action

When energy is transferred, we call it **work done**. Work is done when a force causes an object to move a certain distance in the direction of that force. Think of pushing a box across a floor – you're doing work on it.
$W = Fd$ (where force $F$ and displacement $d$ are in the same direction)
**Power** is how quickly this energy transfer or work happens. A powerful engine can do a lot of work in a short amount of time, indicating a high rate of energy transfer.

Finally, **efficiency** tells us how effectively a system converts input energy into useful output energy. No real system is 100% efficient because some energy is always 'lost' to unwanted forms, typically heat or sound.

items: Work done is energy transferred by a force causing displacement.
Work requires displacement *in the direction of* the force.
Power measures the rate of energy transfer or work done.
Efficiency compares useful output energy to total input energy.
Real systems always have efficiency less than 100% due to energy losses.

sections.question:
A 2.0 kg ball is dropped from a height of 5.0 m.
(a) Calculate its gravitational potential energy at the start.
(b) Assuming no air resistance, calculate its speed just before hitting the ground.
(c) If, due to air resistance, its actual speed just before hitting the ground is 8.5 m/s, calculate the work done against air resistance. (Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$).
sections.solution:
1.  (a) Initial GPE: $GPE = mgh = 2.0 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 5.0 \text{ m} = 98.1 \text{ J}$.
2.  (b) By conservation of energy (no air resistance): Initial GPE = Final KE.
    $98.1 \text{ J} = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = \frac{1}{2}(2.0 \text{ kg})v^2$
    $98.1 = v^2$
    $v = \sqrt{98.1} \approx 9.90 \text{ m/s}$.
3.  (c) With air resistance: Initial GPE = Final KE + Work done against air resistance.
    Initial GPE = $98.1 \text{ J}$.
    Actual Final KE = $\frac{1}{2}mv_{actual}^2 = \frac{1}{2}(2.0 \text{ kg})(8.5 \text{ m/s})^2 = 1.0 \times 72.25 = 72.25 \text{ J}$.
    Work done against air resistance = Initial GPE - Actual Final KE
    Work done against air resistance = $98.1 \text{ J} - 72.25 \text{ J} = 25.85 \text{ J}$.

sections.content: Always state the principle of energy conservation clearly in problem-solving. Remember to account for all forms of energy, especially energy losses due to resistive forces like air resistance or friction, as these are common pitfalls that examiners look for.

sections.label: Mark a past-paper question on this topic

faq.q: Why is it impossible for a machine to be 100% efficient?
faq.a: In any real-world energy conversion, some energy is always dissipated or 'lost' to the surroundings in non-useful forms, most commonly as heat due to friction or air resistance, and sometimes as sound. This means useful output energy will always be less than total input energy.

faq.q: When should I use $W = Fd$ versus $W = Fd \cos \theta$ for work done?
faq.a: You use $W = Fd$ when the force applied is exactly in the same direction as the displacement. You use $W = Fd \cos \theta$ when the force and displacement are at an angle $\theta$ to each other, as it correctly accounts for only the component of the force acting along the direction of motion. For Cambridge AS Physics, $W=Fd$ is usually sufficient if the context implies collinear force and displacement.

faq.q: Can gravitational potential energy ever be negative?
faq.a: Yes, GPE can be negative if your chosen reference point for zero potential energy is *above* the object's current position. Since only the *change* in GPE is physically significant, the absolute value doesn't matter as long as your reference point is consistent throughout the problem.
